name: Creation Stories Around the World
description: Hear or read creation stories from multiple traditions side by side: Genesis (Judaism/Christianity), the Rig Veda hymn of creation (Hinduism), the Quran on creation (Islam), the Popol Vuh (Maya), Aboriginal Dreamtime, and others. Compare: What comes first? Who or what creates? What is the role of humans? What patterns appear across traditions?
materials: age-appropriate versions of creation stories from at least 4 traditions
learning_objectives: Recognize that many cultures have creation stories
Identify common themes (order from chaos, role of water, divine speech) across traditions
Understand that creation stories express deep questions about origins and meaning
variations: Draw your favorite creation story
Act out creation stories in small groups
Write your own creation myth inspired by themes across traditions
Compare creation stories to scientific accounts of the Big Bang

name: Moral Tales Comparison -- Wisdom Across Traditions
description: Read or hear moral tales from different religious traditions: a parable from Jesus, a Jataka tale from Buddhism, a story from the Hadith, a tale from the Panchatantra, a Sufi story, an indigenous teaching story. What moral lesson does each teach? Are the lessons similar or different? How does each tradition use stories to teach ethics?
materials: moral tales from at least 4 traditions
learning_objectives: Understand that many traditions use stories to teach moral lessons
Compare moral teachings across traditions
Recognize common ethical themes (kindness, honesty, compassion, humility)
variations: Retell a moral tale in your own words
Compare to a modern story with a similar lesson
Write a moral tale using themes from multiple traditions

name: World Religions Passport -- Virtual Travel
description: Create a 'passport' and 'travel' to learn about different world religions. Each stop includes: the tradition's name and origin, a key symbol, a core belief, a major celebration, and a sacred space. Younger learners use images and simple facts; older learners add historical context and internal diversity. Stamp your passport at each stop.
materials: images of symbols, sacred spaces, and celebrations
learning_objectives: Name and briefly describe the world's major religious traditions
Identify key symbols, celebrations, and sacred spaces
Understand that religious diversity exists within and between traditions
variations: Virtual field trip using video tours of sacred spaces
Create a digital passport with multimedia
Interview someone from a tradition you visited

name: Religion Timeline Builder
description: Build a visual timeline showing the origins and major developments of the world's religions. When did each tradition begin? What are its key historical events (splits, reformations, expansions, encounters with other traditions)? How do the timelines overlap and interact? What surprises you about the chronology?
materials: timeline template (paper roll, digital tool, or wall display)
date cards for major events in religious history
learning_objectives: Understand the historical origins and chronology of major world religions
Recognize that religions have changed and developed over time
See how religious histories intersect and influence each other
variations: Focus on a single region and its religious history
Add political and cultural events to the timeline for context
Create an interactive digital timeline

name: Sacred Text Reading Circle
description: Read short passages from different sacred texts on a common theme (e.g., compassion, justice, creation, suffering, the golden rule). Discuss: What genre is this passage? Who is the audience? What does it mean in its original context? How do different communities interpret it? How does it compare to passages from other traditions on the same theme?
materials: curated excerpts from sacred texts on a common theme
learning_objectives: Read sacred text passages with attention to genre and context
Understand that interpretation varies across communities
Compare how different traditions address the same theme through their texts
variations: Compare multiple translations of the same passage
Read aloud in the tradition's original style
Write a personal response connecting the passage to your own experience

name: Parable & Metaphor Analysis
description: Examine parables and metaphors from multiple traditions: the Good Samaritan (Christianity), the Blind Men and the Elephant (Buddhism/Hinduism/Jainism/Sufism), the Cave allegory (Plato, used in Islamic philosophy), Rumi's poetry (Sufism). What makes a story a parable? How do metaphors convey theological ideas? Why do teachers across traditions use stories instead of propositions?
materials: parable texts from at least 3 traditions
learning_objectives: Understand parables and metaphors as theological teaching tools
Analyze how metaphors convey complex ideas about the divine or ethical life
Compare the use of figurative language across religious traditions
variations: Write your own parable to convey a moral or theological idea
Compare religious parables to secular fables
Create visual art representing a parable's meaning

name: Golden Rule Across Traditions
description: Discover the Golden Rule as it appears in many traditions: 'Do unto others...' (Christianity), 'What is hateful to you, do not do to your neighbor' (Judaism), 'No one of you is a believer until he desires for his brother what he desires for himself' (Islam), 'Hurt not others in ways that you yourself would find hurtful' (Buddhism), 'This is the sum of duty: do not do to others what would cause pain if done to you' (Hinduism). Why does this principle appear everywhere? Are the formulations identical or subtly different?
materials: Golden Rule quotations from at least 6 traditions
art supplies for creating a Golden Rule poster
learning_objectives: Recognize that the Golden Rule appears across many religious traditions
Compare positive and negative formulations of the principle
Discuss why this ethical principle is so widespread
variations: Create a multilingual Golden Rule poster
Debate whether the formulations are truly the same
Apply the Golden Rule to a current ethical dilemma from multiple perspectives

name: Religion in the News -- Current Events Analysis
description: Collect news articles that involve religion (politics, conflict, community service, holidays, legal cases, art, science debates). Analyze: How is religion portrayed? Is the reporting accurate and fair? What background knowledge would help the reader understand the story? Where does the article get religion right or wrong? How would a religiously literate person read this differently?
materials: current news articles involving religion (from reputable sources)
learning_objectives: Apply religious literacy to current events
Evaluate media portrayals of religion for accuracy and fairness
Recognize how religious literacy changes understanding of world events
variations: Compare coverage of the same event across different news sources
Write a 'religiously literate' version of a news story
Present a weekly religion-in-the-news briefing

name: Interfaith Panel Simulation
description: Each participant is assigned a religious tradition to represent on a panel discussing a shared question: What gives life meaning? How should we treat strangers? What happens after death? How do we respond to suffering? Research your assigned tradition thoroughly. Present its perspective accurately and respectfully. Listen to other panelists. Find common ground and acknowledge genuine differences.
materials: panel discussion rules (respectful listening, no caricature)
learning_objectives: Represent a religious tradition's perspective accurately and respectfully
Engage in structured interfaith dialogue
Identify common ground and genuine differences across traditions
variations: Panel on a contemporary ethical issue (environment, AI, poverty)
Written position papers instead of verbal panel
Follow-up with actual interfaith community members

name: Common Ground Mapping
description: Choose a theological or ethical theme (compassion, justice, stewardship of the earth, treatment of the poor, the nature of truth). Research what 4-5 different traditions teach about this theme. Create a visual map showing areas of overlap (common ground) and areas of genuine difference. Reflect: Why is there so much common ground? Why do genuine differences persist? Can people disagree deeply and still cooperate?
materials: large paper or digital canvas for mapping
markers or digital tools for visual mapping
learning_objectives: Identify common ethical and theological ground across traditions
Acknowledge genuine, irreducible differences without dismissing them
Develop nuanced understanding of interreligious agreement and disagreement
variations: Focus on a practical issue (hunger, environment, peace) rather than abstract theology
Present your map to the group and invite critique
Compare your map to actual interfaith statements or documents

name: Sacred Space Virtual Field Trip
description: Take a virtual tour of sacred spaces from different traditions: a mosque, church, synagogue, Hindu temple, Buddhist temple, gurdwara, Shinto shrine, and indigenous sacred site. What do you notice about each space? How is it designed for worship or meditation? What symbols and art do you see? How does the architecture reflect the tradition's theology?
materials: virtual tour links or videos of sacred spaces
learning_objectives: Recognize different types of sacred spaces across traditions
Understand how architecture and art reflect theological ideas
Develop respectful curiosity about spaces sacred to others
variations: Visit a local sacred space in person (with permission and guidance)
Design a sacred space for an imaginary tradition
Compare sacred spaces to secular spaces designed for reflection

name: Ethics Case Study -- Multiple Religious Perspectives
description: Present an ethical dilemma (environmental stewardship, economic justice, forgiveness after harm, end-of-life decisions, treatment of refugees). Research how at least 3 religious traditions and 1 secular ethical framework would approach the dilemma. Present each perspective fairly. Discuss where they agree, where they differ, and what values drive each approach.
materials: research guides for religious perspectives on the topic
secular ethical frameworks (utilitarian, deontological, virtue ethics) for comparison
learning_objectives: Apply religious ethical reasoning to a concrete dilemma
Compare religious and secular approaches to the same moral question
Understand that ethical reasoning is shaped by underlying worldview commitments
variations: Written position papers from each perspective
Debate format with assigned positions
Follow up with how interfaith organizations actually address this issue
